prompt: Design a team topology for a microservices migration with 30 developers currently organized in 3 feature teams working on a monolith. We need to move to 12 microservices over 18 months.
assertions: classifies teams using Team Topologies types (stream-aligned, platform, enabling, complicated-subsystem)
proposes ≥4 stream-aligned teams mapped to business value streams
recommends a platform team for shared infrastructure (CI/CD, observability, service mesh)
includes an enabling team for migration coaching with a defined exit timeline
designs interaction modes between all team pairs (collaboration, X-as-a-Service, facilitating)
assesses cognitive load per proposed team and flags any team owning >2 domains
addresses Conway's Law alignment between proposed team boundaries and target microservice boundaries
provides a phased team formation plan — not all teams created at once

prompt: Team Phoenix owns the payments domain, the notifications service, and the audit logging pipeline. They also handle on-call for 8 production services. They have 6 developers and are consistently missing sprint commitments. Assess their cognitive load.
assertions: scores domain complexity across all 3 domains (payments, notifications, audit)
quantifies technology diversity across owned services
flags operational burden from on-call across 8 services as unsustainable
calculates a composite cognitive load score exceeding the sustainable threshold
classifies Team Phoenix as Red/overloaded status
recommends specific load-shedding actions (e.g., transfer notifications to another team, extract audit to platform)
identifies that 3 unrelated domains in a single team violates Team Topologies principles
estimates capacity recovered per recommended action

prompt: Our checkout team and payments team have been in collaboration mode for 9 months building the new payment gateway integration. Design a plan to evolve this interaction to X-as-a-Service.
assertions: flags that 9-month collaboration exceeds recommended timebox (≤6 weeks for discovery, ≤12 weeks for complex integration)
defines a concrete API contract between checkout and payments teams
specifies SLA expectations for the payment gateway API (latency, availability, error budget)
designs a phased transition plan with measurable milestones
includes consumer-driven contract testing as a transition prerequisite
defines support channels and escalation paths post-transition
identifies risks of the transition (knowledge silos, untested edge cases)
sets a hard deadline for collaboration termination with accountability

prompt: We are migrating from a monolith to microservices. Currently 4 teams all commit to the same monolith repository. Our target architecture has 8 bounded contexts. Audit Conway's Law alignment and recommend a team restructuring plan.
assertions: identifies the current state as a Conway's Law violation (4 teams, 1 shared codebase)
maps the 8 bounded contexts to potential team boundaries
detects split ownership patterns in the monolith (multiple teams touching same modules)
quantifies misalignment impact with observable symptoms (merge conflicts, deployment coupling, lead time)
proposes a team restructuring plan that aligns team boundaries with bounded contexts
recommends a strangler fig pattern or similar incremental extraction strategy
includes a platform team recommendation for shared infrastructure concerns
provides a Mermaid diagram or visual overlay of team-to-architecture mapping
